Two-stage YOLO object detection and classification with cross-validation for NZ carabids

Aaron Harmer

2025-11-13

Introduction

This report presents the results of a two-stage YOLO model system designed for carabid identification. The classification model was trained and evaluated using k-fold cross-validation to provide robust performance estimates with confidence intervals. This approach ensures more reliable assessment of model generalisation compared to traditional single train-test splits.

Methodology

Model Architecture

Stage 1: Object Detection

  • Model: YOLOv11n (nano version for efficiency)
  • Task: Detect carabids (thorax and elytra only) in images
  • Output: Bounding boxes around detected carabids
  • Single-class detection (all individuals treated as one class)

Stage 2: Classification with Cross-Validation

  • Model: YOLOv11n-cls (classification variant)
  • Task: Classify detected carabids into genus
  • Training: 5-fold cross-validation for robust performance estimation
  • Evaluation: Common held-out test set evaluated across all folds
  • Output: Generic classification with confidence scores and performance metrics

Cross-Validation Approach

The classification dataset was split using stratified 5-fold cross-validation:

  1. Stratified Splitting: Each class maintains proportional representation across all folds, ensuring balanced evaluation
  2. Independent Models: 5 separate models were trained, one for each fold
  3. Common Test Set: A held-out test set (if applicable) was used to evaluate all models consistently
  4. Aggregated Metrics: Performance metrics are reported as mean ± standard deviation across all folds

This approach provides:

  • Robust Performance Estimates: Multiple evaluations reduce the impact of lucky/unlucky data splits
  • Confidence Intervals: Standard deviations indicate model stability and consistency
  • Detection of Overfitting: High variance across folds suggests potential overfitting or data issues
  • Better Generalisation Assessment: More representative of real-world performance

Training Configuration

Training configuration for each model stage
Stage Model Approach Epochs Batch_Size Image_Size Optimizer Dropout Single_Class
Detection YOLOv11n Single Train/Val/Test Split 30 64 640 AdamW N/A Yes
Classification YOLOv11n-cls 5-Fold Cross-Validation 30 92 640 AdamW 0.2 No

All images were pre-processed before training by resizing to 640x640 pixels and adjusting the exposure by contrast stretching. The classification image set was augmented by horizontal flipping and generating random amounts of motion blur.

Cross-Validation Benefits and Insights

Why Cross-Validation?

This analysis used 5-fold cross-validation instead of a single train-test split for several important reasons:

  1. Robust Performance Estimates: By training 5 independent models, we obtain multiple performance measurements, allowing us to report metrics with confidence intervals (mean ± standard deviation).

  2. Reduced Bias from Data Splitting: A single train-test split might be lucky or unlucky. Cross-validation ensures every image is used for validation exactly once, providing a more representative assessment.

  3. Detection of Instability: High standard deviation across folds would indicate model instability or data quality issues. Our low variance confirms model robustness.

  4. Better Use of Limited Data: All available data contributes to both training and validation across the folds, maximizing the information extracted from the dataset.

Cross-Validation Quality Metrics

Cross-validation quality assessment
Metric Value
Mean Accuracy 0.9929
Standard Deviation 0.0023
Coefficient of Variation 0.23%
Performance Range 0.0065
Quality Assessment Excellent (CV < 2%)

Conclusions and Recommendations

Key Findings

  1. Classification Performance: The YOLOv11n-cls classification model achieved an overall accuracy of 0.9929 ± 0.0023 across 5 folds in genus identification.

  2. Cross-Validation Consistency: With a coefficient of variation of 0.23% the model demonstrates excellent consistency across different data splits, indicating robust generalisation.

  3. Training Efficiency: Both models converged effectively within 30 epochs, with the classification model showing consistent convergence patterns across all folds.

  4. Confidence Calibration: The model shows well-calibrated confidence scores, with correct predictions generally having higher confidence than incorrect ones.

Model Reliability

The cross-validation approach provides several insights into model reliability:

  • Performance Consistency: Accuracy across folds ranges from 0.9884 to 0.9949 (range: 0.0065)
    • This narrow range indicates the model is highly stable and not overly sensitive to the specific train/validation split
  • High-Performing Classes: 72 classes achieved F1 scores ≥ 0.95, demonstrating excellent discrimination

Recommendations

Based on the cross-validation results, recommend:

  1. Deployment Strategy:
    • Use the best-performing fold model for deployment, or
    • Implement an ensemble approach averaging predictions across all 5 models for even more robust predictions
  2. Data Collection Priorities:
    • Focus additional data collection efforts on classes showing lower F1 scores
    • Ensure new data maintains the quality standards of the training set
  3. Model Updates:
    • Re-evaluate using cross-validation when new data becomes available
    • The current model’s consistency suggests it will generalize well to similar data

Technical Specifications

Technical specifications
Component Specification
YOLO Version YOLOv11 (Ultralytics)
Framework PyTorch
Python Environment Python 3.8+ with Ultralytics
Image Input Size 640×640 pixels
Inference Device GPU/CPU Compatible
Model Weights Format PyTorch (.pt)
Cross-Validation 5-fold stratified
Evaluation Approach Common test set across all folds
